channel.title: Climate Risk (Google Alerts)
channel.description: Últimos artigos de Climate Risk (Google Alerts)

item.title: The quiet death of conscious consumerism, from Everlane and Allbirds to Beyond Meat
item.description:
When Everlane burst onto the apparel scene in 2010, its “radical transparency” on matters of pricing and sourcing, coupled with its emphasis on a clean, modern aesthetic and storytelling about the people and places behind its product, made it a hit with millennials seeking chic wardrobe basics, along with the warm and fuzzy feeling of being a responsible consumer.

Those millennials professing to “vote” with their dollars placed greater weight on sustainability, authenticity, and companies being good corporate citizens than their elders did and saw such virtues as perhaps just as important as the functionality or beauty of the products themselves. Tapping into that zeitgeist helped Everlane sell a lot of product at higher prices than its fast-fashion counterparts—and win the attention of major investors, including VC firms such as Kleiner Perkins and Khosla, as well as LVMH-backed L Catterton.

So the news last week that Shein—a hyper fast-fashion brand whose name has become synonymous with high-velocity consumerism—bought the now struggling, debt-laden Everlane for $100 million (well below its peak of $600 million), set off a slew of think pieces about the demise of “conscious consumerism.” And indeed, it adds to the mounting evidence that an ethical stance, without a clear value proposition and raison d’être, is not enough for a brand to succeed these days.

Everlane is not the only relic of the ethical consumption era to find itself in ignominious circumstances. In March, Allbirds, once beloved by the Silicon Valley set for its sustainably made wool sneakers, said it would sell itself to a brand management company for $39 million, or 1% of its peak value. Then, after years of making its eco-friendliness the very center of its brand messaging and identity, the sneaker-maker left analysts befuddled with the announcement that it would re-invent itself as an AI infrastructure company.

Another brand to emerge from this era of eco-friendly mindfulness, the plant-based food company Beyond Meat, sought to tap into consumers’ desire for the taste of meat without the guilt of its environmental costs or the ethical tradeoffs of large-scale agriculture. Once touted as a pioneer of the high-tech future of food, it saw revenue plunge as consumers have either decided they prefer real meat or balked at the higher prices Beyond Meat was charging. The company recently dropped “Meat” from its name and entered new categories such as protein drinks to tap into consumers’ growing interest in protein-based products.

All three companies tapped into a zeitgeist, but appear to have forgotten that products have to offer consumers more than a feeling of virtuousness to build an enduring business.

In the case of Everlane, there was nothing particularly special about its generic but well-made clothing in tastefully bland colors. The original proposition was that consumers would be willing to pay more for products made ethically and sustainably. But in an uncertain “K-shaped” economy, it turns out that many shoppers are not going to shell out for vague promises about an item’s origins. Meanwhile, other brands with similar aesthetics—including Uniqlo, Quince or even some lines at Walmart—have become formidable competitors without the virtue-signaling.

“The brands ran out of steam when their promises ran afoul of the economics of consumer preference,” Forrester Research vice president and principal analyst Dipanjan Chatterjee wrote this week about Everlane and Allbirds. “The consumer gap between stated values and revealed behavior became painfully clear.” He noted that his firm’s research has shown time and time again that factors such as price, convenience, reliability, and design are more important to consumers than sustainability.

Over time, shoppers have also grown more skeptical of the idea that they can buy their way to a better world. The promise of conscious consumerism never really grappled with the fact that the core problem is overconsumption itself—and global demand for clothes, shoes, and cheap products has continued to climb. Many consumers now seem to grasp that choosing the “right” sneaker or burger might offer a personal sense of virtue, but it does little to deliver the kind of systemic change needed to tackle climate risk, exploitative labor practices, or the depletion of natural resources.

It’s not that consumers don’t care about environmental stewardship and fair wages for the people that make their clothing and food—but the Everlane saga suggests that they don’t see ethical and sustainable practices, in themselves, as a reason to pay a premium.

Sign up here.
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Southern California chemical tank at risk of exploding as 40000 residents are ordered to evacuate
item.description: Authorities are bracing for the possibility that a damaged chemical tank at a facility in Southern California could leak or explode.
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Climate Change Threatens Global Plant Species as Habitats Shrink - U.S. News & World Report
item.description: They found that 7% to ‌16% could lose more than 90% of their range, placing them at high risk of extinction. Examples include Catalina ironwood ...
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: First Nations look to flip the script on Canada's power buildout
item.description:
As a child, Kwatuuma Cole Sayers watched his mother turn the tables on how consultation and development take place with a First Nation.

He could not have known then it would lead him here — trying to help First Nations get ahead of Canada’s next energy buildout, before decisions are made about the lines crossing their lands.

The story about his mother goes that, 20 years ago, Port Alberni was looking for industry. There was talk of a smelter plant — the kind of heavy industry that promised jobs and economic diversification, but came with environmental costs Hupacasath First Nation did not want in its territory.

His mother, Judith Sayers, then chief of the Hupacasath First Nation, was not interested in simply opposing development. She wanted another answer.

Clean energy became that answer.

Sayers says his mother saw renewable energy as an industry that could align with Hupacasath values — something that could generate revenue and build relationships with other First Nations, the City of Port Alberni, private industry, and the province, without bringing the same environmental harms into their territory.

“ I really got to see my mom and my community get behind this project and saying, ‘This is what we want to do. We’re going go out and learn how to do it,’” he recalls.

It was not an easy sell back then.

At an industry conference, Sayers says, one man — who “will remain unnamed ” — told his mother First Nations would never build renewable energy projects because they were too complex.

His mom took that as a challenge. She dug into the process and learned how run-of-river hydro projects are built. She found suppliers, funders and pulled together partners.

Sayers remembers being a kid, sitting in their favourite local Chinese restaurant, Beijing, while his mother ordered food and made business deals across the table.

That was where he saw it happen.

Legwork. Paperwork. Dinner conversations and persistence.

The result was a small six-and-a-half megawatt project near Port Alberni.

Small by today’s standards, maybe, but big in its implications.

”Being able to build it on our terms and go out and to do it, and really show other nations that it can be done, and that’s what I really appreciate. What my mom did was that she showed other nations that it’s possible to go out and build a project on your own.”

These days, Sayers runs the Indigenous Power Coalition (IPC), a not-for-profit agency that consults with First Nations on transmission lines.

With Canada about to embark on a national electrification plan that will double energy output and infrastructure by 2050, some lines and towers will need to run through Indigenous lands.

Sayer says First Nations leaders should not wait to be consulted.

Instead, they should – like his mom – flip the script and develop their plans first, and present them to the government and industry.

The big build

The governments of B.C. and Alberta were already in talks about linking their electrical grids, building new steel towers, called interties, to connect them.

For just over a year, Sayers and the IPC had been meeting with chiefs to help them develop their own plans for the potential rollout.

It’s a complicated process.

What lands should be protected; what services, jobs, businesses, energy sources should they develop, and do they want to own a piece of the grid? What demands do they have to clean up for legacy infrastructure that was put on their lands in the past without consultation and how to navigate all the regulatory red tape around them?

It is in early stages, but if Sayer’s idea works, chiefs will form coalitions, develop their plan first, and non-Indigenous governments and businesses will then become the consultees.

IPC’s role is just to provide neutral information about the options available and how they work, says Sayers nothing is “pre-baked.”

IPC is Indigenous-led and Sayers is the founder. It’s funded by foundations and not connected to any government.

“ We want to flip that on its head and tell these utilities that First Nations want to have greater control on how these potential lines are designed, built, and governed. And that also includes having First Nations being able to have a say in what type of generation will be connecting to the grid, and the type of electrons will be moving along,” he said.

In some projects, First Nations have gone beyond benefit agreements.

The ownership model offers First Nations more than a role during construction. It gives them a chance to buy into the power line itself.

That usually means taking on debt at the start, with early payments used to pay it down. But if the deal works, the community is left with a long-term revenue stream from infrastructure that may operate for decades.

In other models, that return is relatively predictable. The First Nation may receive a regular payment tied to the size or capacity of the line, not necessarily how much power moves through it. That means the project can operate more like a steady payment to the First Nation rather than a high-risk business bet, though the details depend on the deal.

In March, the project got bigger than just B.C. and Alberta – it morphed into a national plan.

That’s when Premier Doug Ford announced an Ontario-led partnership with several provinces and territories to build new transmission lines and interties so Canada’s electricity grids can better connect, share power and meet rising demand.

The reason tying provincial power grids together matters is that the current system is inefficient.

Each province mostly operates like an island, serving its own population and planning for worst-case scenarios, such as handling peak demand, on its own. That means provinces have expensive backup systems and even those can fail.

Yukon offers a stark example.

In December 2025, electricity demand in the territory hit record levels twice. Its hydro system could not produce enough power during peak winter demand, so Yukon had to bring in diesel generators just to keep the lights and heat on.

If provinces join their grids, they can get the electricity they need from another province via transmission lines.

Meanwhile, when provinces have extra power, much of it still flows south.

According to the Canada Energy Regulator, Canada exported about 35.64 terawatt-hours of electricity in 2024 — roughly six per cent of its total generation. To put that in perspective, it would be enough to power the country of Ireland for a year, according to figures from Ireland’s Sustainable Energy Authority.

Blake Shaffer is an associate professor at the University of Calgary, specializing in electricity markets. He is also an advisor to the Indigenous Power Coalition.

Like Sayers, Shaffer has a family history tied to clean energy. His dad, also an energy economist, testified at the Canadian Senate in the early 1980s, advocating for a connected western power grid.

“It’s been something that’s been on the radar for a long time. It’s something that most folks who step back and look at a high level say there are tremendous benefits of linking regions. You’ve got diversity in resources, diversity of demands. Just like any gains from trade, you benefit from being able to share resources rather than just doubling things up,” said Shaffer.

Shaffer says interties have long been difficult because electricity is controlled in provincial silos, with regulatory, commercial and political barriers between them — but Trump’s rhetoric about Canada pushed governments to look inward and got provinces talking seriously about lowering those barriers.

Then, last week, Prime Minister Mark Carney unveiled the government’s electrification plan called Powering Canada Strong: A National Strategy for an Electrified Canadian Economy. It proposes to not only support the uniting of provincial power grids but to double power generation and electrical energy infrastructure by 2050.

The plan promises to consult Indigenous Nations along the path of the interties on the development and to help those who are on diesel get connected to the grid, where 80 per cent of Canada’s electricity comes from non-emitting sources.

But for Sayers, the point is to get ahead of the old-style consultation process — where governments and companies develop the plan first, then bring it to First Nations after the major decisions have already been made.

“It’s got to be with the First Nations having significant control over it,” he said.

Because there are only a few routes the interties can take, given the landscape and existing infrastructure, Indigenous rights holders will have significant power over how plans are implemented, he added.

For some provinces, that may mean changing how they think about hydro ownership to give First Nations more control, especially if existing provincial laws require them to maintain sole ownership of power transmission infrastructure through crown corporations.

Quebec passed Bill 13 (An Act respecting the Hertel–New York interconnection line) in 2023 to permit the Mohawk Council of Kahnawà:ke to co-own transmission lines passing through their territory to the U.S..

B.C. also recently amended its legislation last fall to allow First Nations to hold an equity stake in the North Coast Transmission Line, said Sayer.

There is no co-ownership at present in Manitoba, but in Ontario, Hydro One has 50/50 Indigenous equity options, and Alberta is a deregulated market with more examples of First Nations having ownership stakes.

The Carney government’s electrification plan goes beyond interties and the power grid, though those are a big part of it.

“Working alongside provinces, territories, Indigenous Peoples, and industry, we will keep reliability high and bills low as we expand clean power for all Canadians and connect our grids from coast to coast to coast,” said Tim Hodgson, minister of Energy and Natural Resources at the time of the announcement.

The plan points to the Nova Scotia–New Brunswick Wasoqonatl Reliability Intertie as one example already underway, and the proposed Iqaluit Nukkiksautiit Hydro Project, led by Nunavut’s first fully Inuit-owned renewable energy developer, as examples.

But it is less clear from the plan how remote communities that are not located near the paths connecting the provincial systems and still need reliable, clean power fit in.

Carney’s Powering Canada Strong plan

The plan is ambitious. It will cost $1 trillion and it is happening in a context of global disruption.

“In a rapidly changing and more volatile world, Canada is taking control of our future,” said Carney when the plan was announced last week.

Globally, all countries are in a race to expand their electricity capacity.

It was always part of the vision for climate change that “clean electricity” would replace fossil fuels. The shift to electric vehicles, heat pumps and cleaner buildings is part of it.

But this isn’t only about how much electricity Canadians will use. Carney has tied Canada’s economic future, especially in a more uncertain trade relationship with the U.S., to becoming an energy superpower.

And that means ensuring Canada has the electricity supply to feed industries that use data centres – bitcoin, artificial intelligence, and mining of critical minerals.

Powering Canada Strong warns that if Canada can’t deliver reliable, affordable power quickly, those investments will go elsewhere.

That makes electricity not just part of the race away from fossil fuels to address the climate emergency, but also the race for economic survival and in the north, there is also a military and sovereignty tie-in.

The investments in the plan include $6 billion to recruit new employees into the energy sector where the industry will need an estimated 28,000 skilled workers by 2028 and 130,000 workers by 2050.

And there is also an announcement that a Nuclear Energy Plan is coming. Currently, Nuclear powers about 15 per cent of Canada’s electricity grid.

But the plan also frames nuclear as more than a domestic energy source. It says Canada can help feed a world hungry for power, using what it calls a “competitive advantage…presenting opportunities for economic growth, enduring international partnerships, and trade diversification.”

The energy business, climate goals and nuclear power

Serena Mendizabal, a Cayuga woman from the Six Nations and climate activist who serves as managing director at Sacred Earth Solar, had been anticipating the plan for months.

When she read it, it fell short of what she expected.

“ I really felt that climate was an afterthought throughout the entirety of the strategy because it was really platformed on economic opportunity that is going to come from electrification and energy growth,” she told Nation to Nation.

Before becoming Canada’s prime minister, Carney spent seven years as governor of the Bank of England, where he warned industry about climate risks and pushed companies to adapt.

In this document, the repeated mantra sounds more like Carney the economist, warning that electrification is needed to make Canada more competitive, she said.

”Throughout the entirety of the strategy, if you actually read it, it goes, ‘This is why this will be good for the economy,’ and then it goes dot, dot, dot, ‘As well as the climate and protecting the environment,’” said Mendizabal. “So I found that really interesting because once again, in hopes of something really transformative, it was not necessarily what we were looking for.”

Last month, Parliament’s Standing Committee on Environment and Sustainable Development completed an eight-month-long study warning that Canada is making bigger climate promises while falling further behind on delivering them.

It noted that 2024 was the hottest year ever recorded globally, the first to pass 1.5 C above pre-industrial levels, and a record year for insured damage from extreme weather in Canada, topping $8.5 billion.

“ When I see what is happening around this electricity strategy and I’m thinking about climate, we really are focusing on instead of, “Oh, we are having major climate change impacts and natural disasters such as flooding, wildfires, drought, atmospheric river,” we’re actually more so looking how do we make sure that this non-emitting electricity sector will actually serve as a backbone of a low carbon economy?” said Mendizabal.

Canada has pledged to cut emissions 40 to 45 per cent below 2005 levels by 2030 and reach net-zero by 2050.

But the environmental committee report said the country is nowhere close.

As of 2023, emissions were only 8.5 per cent below 2005 levels, and Ottawa’s own projections show Canada on track for just a 28 per cent cut by 2030. In other words, the targets are written down, but the plan to reach them still is not.

The electrification plan promises that if Canada moves aggressively, electrification could cut emissions by as much as five times the electricity sector’s current emissions, with even bigger gains possible as technology improves and becomes cheaper.

However, Mendizabal said the plan is overly focused on cutting greenhouse gas emissions as its primary goal, treating carbon cuts as the sole measure of success.

For example, she says the strategy treats nuclear as clean energy because it can cut emissions, without seriously grappling with the waste it leaves behind or the impacts of uranium mining on the land.

“When I think about it, and I think this is what we’re really going towards when it comes to non-emitting, this is what the transformative solutions that we’re working towards, it still is at the expense of Indigenous peoples in our territories, and it’ll still bring destruction to our homelands,” she said.

And she is not alone.

First Nations have been cautious participants in the nuclear energy conversation, balancing the push to cut emissions against concerns about waste, uranium mining, and long-term impacts on land and water.

Ontario is now moving into new territory. North America’s first small modular reactor is planned for Darlington, just east of Toronto, as the province looks for new ways to meet rising electricity demand.

The Michi Saagiig Anishinaabeg Nations of Alderville, Curve Lake, Hiawatha and Scugog Island First Nations issued a statement that they “recognize the significance of this development and the importance of ensuring that our Nations’ voices and interests are meaningfully included as it moves forward,” but remain committed to ensuring the plan protects the land and water, while the community benefits.

”I’ve already seen many petitions going out from communities nearby of how this will impact water sources near. And also thinking beyond, it’s one thing to have clean production, but from extraction to disposal, nuclear energy is dangerous, and nuclear energy is not clean or renewable,“ said Mendizabal.

“We saw in the strategy that Canada has one of the world’s largest uranium reserves in northern Saskatchewan. Sacred Earth has actually worked directly in northern Saskatchewan to put solar projects up, and this has impacted lands. This has impacted their hunting and their country foods, and it’s also really monopolized the job market in northern Saskatchewan for people,” she said.

Sheldon Wuttunee, president and chief executive officer of the Saskatchewan First Nations Natural Resource Centre of Excellence, told the natural resources committee earlier this year that uranium has already created jobs and supply-chain opportunities for First Nations in northern Saskatchewan through relationships with companies such as Cameco, Orano and NexGen.

He added that the possible rollout of small modular reactors makes uranium even more important — and more complicated.

First Nations, he said, need the capacity and technical knowledge to make fully informed decisions about nuclear development, especially given the industry’s mixed history and reactions.

“The path forward is to ensure that we’re bringing on adequate capacity to fully understand and make fully informed decisions when it comes to small modular reactor deployment here in the province. Like anywhere, I think across the globe there are mixed reactions when it comes to nuclear, based on historical experience and past understanding,” he told the committee.

Meanwhile, the Assembly of First Nations is holding a meeting next week on Canada’s proposals for nuclear waste management. In Ontario, First Nations have warned that a proposed deep geological repository for used nuclear fuel could put land, water, air, sacred sites and the Great Lakes at risk.

Kebaowek First Nation’s fight over the proposed nuclear waste facility at Chalk River, near the Kichi Sibi, or Ottawa River, has become one of the fiercest legal tests in Canada over whether Indigenous consent includes the right to say “no” — and how far the United Nations Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples can be pushed in Canadian law.

That is the tension running through the nuclear debate. In Saskatchewan, uranium has brought jobs, income and business opportunities to some First Nations. But for others, nuclear is still being counted as clean because it does not emit carbon at the point of generation, while the burdens of mining, waste and risk remain tied to Indigenous lands.

More:

Leaked tape: ‘If the provinces aren’t trying hard enough, we’ll bring them up’, Carney tells chiefs

First Nations must define economic reconciliation at fall meeting, says financial expert

Consultation and economic reconciliation

Carney has often cited the federal Indigenous Loan Guarantee Program as evidence that Ottawa is trying to move beyond consultation and into ownership. He has doubled the program from $5 billion to $10 billion.

It works a bit like a federal co-signor, helping Nations borrow money for business and infrastructure projects that could generate returns.

The Powering Canada Strong also includes two key commitments on consultation, promising to create federal economic zones through regional impact assessments, in consultation with Indigenous Peoples, and to set up a “Crown Consultation Hub to strengthen Crown consultation on project reviews and coordinate one Indigenous consultation process, per community, per project.”

Nation to Nation reached out to the Assembly of First Nations for its response to the plan’s references to consultation, but did not hear back by our publication deadline.

However, National Chief Cindy Woodhouse Neepinak has made regular appearances before parliamentary committees outlining the need for better consultation on energy projects.

“To become a global energy superpower, as is Canada’s ambition, First Nations ownership and partnership must be prioritized,” she told the Natural Resources committee in March.

“First Nations must lead the conversation on energy development in their territories, ensuring that inherent and Treaty rights are upheld and that our voices guide policies, partnerships and sustainable practice in alignment with Canada’s obligation under the United Nations Declaration on Indigenous Rights.”

Consultation has been a sore spot with First Nations leaders since Bill C-5, the One Canadian Economy Act, was rushed into law in two weeks with no consultation.

It has frequently come up in presentations on other matters and in other evidence given before parliament. As recently as last week, Nishnawbe Aski Nation Grand Chief Alvin Fiddler reminded the Standing Committee on Indigenous and Northern Affairs that he had to “sit out in the hall” and was not allowed into the room while the committee heard testimony on C-5.

MP Billy Morin, the Conservative Indigenous Services critic, said consultation is a weak point in the strategy.

”When I think about the communities in rural remote areas and the overlap of how many of them are missing out on clean water, a lot of them are now missing out on being connected to the grid and having reliable electricity in their own small communities… but there are no actual tangible plans,” he said.

“I think it’s a lot of the government saying a lot of things, saying buzzwords for Indigenous reconciliation, but no actual tangible plan to include them, and back that up against their record on water. I don’t have faith that the government is doing the right thing when it comes to, uh, supporting those communities.”

Meanwhile, the Kebaowek First Nation has been developing its own laws aligned with UNDRIP while awaiting the latest court decision on the proposed nuclear waste site.

It’s similar to the position taken by Sayers on hydro interties, where he believes projects would be designed by Indigenous communities first.

“ I think to some extent we want to educate others on how to engage because we feel we’re the experts, and we want to raise the bar,” said Sayers.
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Endangered sawfish at risk from plan to double water extraction from WA's Fitzroy River ...
item.description:
Conservationists fear a government plan to double groundwater extraction from the Martuwarra Fitzroy River catchment in Western Australia could jeopardise threatened sawfish populations.

The untamed river, which flows 700km through the Kimberley to King Sound, is considered the last stronghold for sawfish globally and is home to four of the world’s five species.

Sawfish, a type of ray named for their chainsaw-like snouts, are “probably one of the most unique looking animals on the planet”, said Dr Leonardo Guida, from the Australian Marine Conservation Society. The river, its estuary and near-shore marine environment are home to largetooth, dwarf, green and narrow sawfish, all protected under national environment laws.

Endangered largetooth sawfish – the largest and most imperilled – are born at the river’s mouth. The young spend several years inhabiting the river, its tributaries and deep aquifer-fed pools before heading out to sea. Adults can reach up to seven metres in size.

“What makes the Fitzroy River incredibly special and unique from a sawfish perspective is that it is the baseline, not just in Australia but globally, of what a relatively untouched sawfish population looks like,” Guida said.

But a state government proposal to increase the groundwater allocation from about 32GL to 75.7GL has raised concern among environmentalists and communities. Groundwater aquifers supply several large pools and wetlands that act as crucial refuges for sawfish and other species during long dry periods.

“The underground water stores that feed these refuge pools are absolutely critical in a landscape that’s so hot and dry,” said Martin Pritchard from Environs Kimberley, which works to protect nature in the region.

“Sawfish won’t survive without these pools, which also provide life support for barramundi, a whole range of other fish, freshwater prawns and big trees and vegetation that sustain birds, possums, bats and insects.

“We’re really concerned that the current proposal by the government is way too much and could cause significant damage to the river and the groundwater-dependent ecosystems.”

The WA government’s draft water plan, now out for consultation, has proposed no additional surface water allocations and no dams on the river. Environs Kimberley supported these aspects of the plan but called for a more precautionary approach to groundwater.

Dr Anne Poelina, the executive chair of the Martuwarra Fitzroy River Council, an alliance of elders and young leaders from traditional owners of the catchment, said the lived experience of Aboriginal people on country was that the river was already stressed.

“Water is precious, it’s a life force, and if living water systems continue to decline it will continue to affect people’s lives and livelihoods as well as the environment, including access to clean drinking water and the rising cost of living,” the Nyikina Warrwa woman said.

Poelina said there were currently too many unknowns – about the connectivity of the system and about how much groundwater there was, and the potential impact of sea level rise – to start allocating groundwater for profit-making activities.

Both communities and the WA Department of Water and Environmental Regulation (DWER) needed a longer timeframe to be able to gather information before the plan was finalised, or any additional licences given, she said.

A similar water allocation plan in the Pilbara had failed, with groundwater aquifers in the region in decline, which didn’t offer much confidence about the plan for the Kimberley.

“These are human issues as well as environmental issues – it does not make us confident when the science and lived experience stories of how these systems are changing right now,” Poelina said. “These are public interest matters. This is going to impact everyone that’s living within this system. We need to proceed with caution and not take risks with the lives of all Kimberley people and our environment.”

The river has national and Aboriginal heritage list status for its outstanding cultural and natural values.

Farmers have sought greater access to the river’s surface and groundwater for agriculture and livestock, in various plans dating back to the 1950s. Recent proposals for unconventional gas developments, known as fracking, could also have implications for groundwater resources.

Dr Ryan Vogwill, a hydrogeologist, said groundwater played an “incredibly important” role supporting the high biodiversity and cultural values of the river ecosystem during dry periods.

“In a big wet season, the whole system floods with water and goes all the way to the coast,” he said. “But in smaller years, when there’s not so much rainfall, the river may only flow a little, or may not flow at all.

“In those periods when the surface water isn’t flowing and hasn’t flowed for some time, groundwater becomes key to the environment persisting and staying healthy.”

Vogwill said interactions between the surface water and different layers of groundwater aquifers were highly complex and more monitoring would help to better understand the system and assess any potential risks – whether from increased use for pastoral industries, or proposals for unconventional gas.

Applicants seeking a licence would need to demonstrate “sustainable groundwater use” and “protect water-dependent ecosystems and sites of ecological, cultural and social significance”, according to the draft plan.

Guardian Australia asked DWER how the groundwater allocation was determined and whether the government would invest in additional monitoring but did not receive a response.
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: The Greenbelt is a 'safe haven' for 70% more species at risk than 2 decades ago, a new report says
item.description: The new study from the environmental charity Ontario Nature looks at available government data on species at risk in 2004, a year before the protected ...
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Who controls the water when Ontario's rivers run high? - Sault Ste. Marie News
item.description:
Damn those dams this spring?

Power and water control dams play a big part in holding back and releasing water. There are advantages to all this infrastructure on the back roads.

Water always goes where it wants to go. This maxim really underlines Mother Nature’s persistence at this time of the year.

It is a force of nature that follows gravity and paths of least resistance. Particularly into basements, across roadways and over bridges. Along the way, there is erosion and damage.

Historically, people-built farms and homes on floodplains because rivers/lakes provided fresh water, flat land for building, and fertile soil. Also, water transportation came first before the railways and highways, henceforth, settlement patterns.

Who’s to blame? There’s a great deal to know. That’s why it is a story to tell.

Why Dams?

We don’t blame labour unions.

Through strikes and bargaining, they were instrumental in establishing the modern two-day weekend and fighting for paid holidays and a standard 40-hour work week.

Unions created leisure time for us. People with camps and cottages wanted the water levels of their lakes regulated. The original Lands and Forests Ontario, now Ministry of Natural Resources (MNR), obliged with a system of water control dams and weirs.

The dams/weirs served to block, store, and manage water flow at key entry and exit points on the watershed, keeping summer cottaging/camp water levels stable.

And then we have an unquenchable thirst for electricity for our devices. Power dams are everywhere, and more are being developed in northern Ontario. The modern hydroelectric water turbine evolved in phases. The foundational modern water turbine was invented by James Francis in 1849, followed by the Pelton wheel in the 1870s. Hydroelectric generation started about 1880. It is a clean source of energy, no doubt about it.

Ontario needs to expand hydroelectric power to meet rising electricity demand, due to population growth, electric vehicle adoption, and industrial expansion. The first shortage is forecasted for 2029, increasing by 75 per cent by 2050.

When the power goes out, we are inconvenienced.

So, there will be more dams.

New projects in early development involve the OPG partnering with the Taykwa Tagamou First Nation and Moose Cree First Nation on early planning for two brand-new hydro stations in the Abitibi and Moose River basins (the Nine Mile Rapids and Grand Rapids projects). Most OPG dams are constantly being upgraded, especially the turbines.

History

It was not too long ago when the news did not include so many natural occurrences like ice storms, wildfires, tornadoes, downbursts and flooding. Not now, though, as climate change is here.

Yes, from tree ring examination, we have had historic floods like Manitoba’s Red River. There have been seven historic floods in the last 350 years: 1997, 1979, 1950, 1852, 1826, 1762 and 1747.

Spring flooding has increased in severity in many other places, as with other natural occurrences.

This year, in northeastern Ontario and other places in the province, there has been high water in numerous places.

AI says: “Flooding in Ontario has become more frequent and severe in the past decade, with significant, record-breaking events occurring in 2017, 2019, and 2023. While historic floods (e.g., 1954 Hurricane Hazel) occurred in the past, climate change is now accelerating extreme weather, leading to higher lake levels and more intense, rapid rain and snow events.” There is a higher frequency of occurrence, and the historical prediction of the one event within the one-hundred-year time span no longer applies.

It’s Complicated

Dams have various purposes: flood management, raising low water levels for navigation and docks (recreation), water supply, habitat creation, historic (logging), and hydro power production. There are also weirs with a fixed crest where water continually flows over the structure. There are fish weirs/ladders too. One is on the St. Mary’s River in the “Soo,” and another in Thunder Bay, where the Current River creates Boulevard Lake.

“AI says there are approximately more than 2,600 dams across Ontario, primarily located in the Great Lakes Basin watersheds.” These include Ontario Power Generation (OPG), federal, Ministry of Natural Resources (MNR), private, First Nations (FN) and conservation authorities’ (CA) dams.

It’s complicated. Like the name of the 2009 comedy movie starring Meryl Streep and Alec Baldwin.

For example, the French River dams complex is a network of three federal dams (Big Chaudière, Portage, and Little Chaudière) operated by Public Services and Procurement Canada to regulate Lake Nipissing water levels. The original log dams were constructed back in 1910, and the purpose was linked to the Georgian Bay Ship Canal scheme.

Nearby is the Okikendawt Hydroelectric dam, a more recent (2015) 10 MW facility jointly owned by the Dokis First Nation and FirstLight Energy.

The dams create power (FN/FirstLight) and hold back water for Lake Nipissing water levels (federal). Below the dams, downstream, the Lower French River is affected before the water reaches Georgian Bay. There are many camps/cottages and small communities like the Municipality of French River – a flooding declaration was issued this year because of upstream dam discharges.

Contact (May 11) was made with Chief Gerry Duquette of Dokis First Nation about this Upper French River dam co-spatial relationship. “At this time, I can’t comment directly on the water levels above or below the dams as Dokis is in negotiations for past grievances with Public Works Canada.”

Also, the Timiskaming dam complex, located on the Ottawa River on the Ontario/Quebec border, this complex consists of two separate dams—one owned by the Canadian federal government and one by Quebec. Downstream at Mattawa is the OPG La Cave (Otto Holden) Dam. Mattawa recently experienced flooding, and back on May 6, 2019. Here is one of my social media posts on one of the flood warning days.

Most large-scale hydroelectric dams on FN traditional territories are built, owned, and operated via equity partnerships between Indigenous communities, the provincial government, and utilities like OPG.

You often see concrete water control dams on the back roads, often with yellow painted railings, red and white warning signs, yellow/orange floating water lines, an overhead boom mechanism with hooks, along with the heavy rectangular stop logs, mostly B.C. Douglas fir, on the deck, to control water levels. Usually, great places to see plunging rapids like the Talon Chutes on the Mattawa River. Cottage and camp owners often complain to the MNR about seasonal water levels. It is a juggling water level management act based on the changing climate and severe weather events.

Mike Fenn of the MNR told me these dams are classified into four categories based on the risk of damage, environmental impact, or loss of life if a failure were to occur.

There is a comprehensive provincial dam inventory with an interactive map.

So, lots of dam structures.

He said the MNR operates and maintains approximately 310 dams across Ontario, including 75 in the Northeast MNR Region and, for example, 15 in the North Bay MNR District alone.

“On average, each MNR district is responsible for about 17 dams. Most ministry-owned dams regulate water levels and flows through the placement or removal of timber stoplogs, while others operate as fixed weirs or use a combination of both systems.”

MNR dams are regularly inspected on a schedule. “Over time, aging and deterioration can affect dam operations and structural integrity and, in severe cases, increase the risk of failure. To support public safety and environmental protection, the ministry maintains an infrastructure plan to assess dams and prioritize repairs or replacements based on risk.

Rehabilitation can extend a dam’s service life by several decades, while full reconstruction can provide a life expectancy of up to 100 years through modern standards and materials.

“The ministry is currently tracking approximately 70 infrastructure projects – including dams, bridges, and roads – at various stages of development, ranging from planning and design to approvals and construction.”

Good to know. Our tax dollars at work on the back roads.

Communication

And then there are the OPG hydro and water control dams. There are 25 hydroelectric generating stations in northern Ontario and more than 100 other control structures. There’s a history of mega hydro projects in northern Ontario with long-lasting ramifications.

Jennifer Stone is OPG’s Senior Media Relations Officer. She said OPG can only impact water levels and flows at their dams and generating stations. “We do this following established regulations – for example, Water Management Plans (WMP).

WMP for many Ontario river systems are developed “as a collaborative process, with input from stakeholders, Rightsholders, municipalities, other dam operators, the MNR, and special interest groups.

“This joint effort between all involved agencies and stakeholders is especially important during the spring freshet, particularly when water flows can be unusually high, due to a heavy snowpack, snow melt from warming temperatures, and precipitation events, as we are seeing in many areas of the province this spring.

Throughout the year, OPG regularly communicates water levels, flow and expected future conditions at OPG dams to regulatory authorities such as the MNR and CAs, as well as other dam operators and Standing Advisory Committees.

“During freshet, the organizations communicate frequently – sometimes as often as daily – to share condition reports, operational practices, and other relevant information.

“We must balance many factors, including public safety, limited reservoir storage capacity, environmental stewardship, and local water management plans and agreements, to determine allowable limits for managing the holding or release of water from our many dams on rivers across Ontario.”

Also, dams must be upgraded. As an example, OPG is undertaking a $178 million refurbishment and expansion project on the Wanapitei River, within the City of Greater Sudbury, targeting two major generating stations (Coniston and Stinson) and the Wanapitei Lake Control Dam (southern outlet).

Another example of coordinating communication comes from the City of North Bay, which was hit by flooding this spring, particularly Chippewa Creek, which flows through the centre of the city.

Spokesperson Gord Young said the Sturgeon-Nipissing-French River (SNF) watershed is managed through a multi-stakeholder, collaborative governance model called the Sturgeon, Nipissing, French River WMP Group. “Their role is to coordinate water level and flow management across Lake Nipissing, the Sturgeon River, and the French River and to work alongside Public Services and Procurement Canada (PSPC), which operates the major control dams in the watershed.”

The SNF water management group meets virtually throughout the year to share information. “They use consultation and information sharing to balance environmental, economic and social needs. The frequency of the meetings increases when there are risks of drought or flood in the watershed.” He identified the members of the SNF group: Federal agencies (PSPC), Provincial agencies (MECP, MNR), Municipal (North Bay, Callander, French River, Temagami), CA (NBMCA), industry (OPG), tourism businesses and Indigenous communities (Nipissing and Dokis FN’s).

“The city’s role is to advocate for local needs (drinking water source protection, shoreline erosion concerns, economic impacts, flood risks to infrastructure). Inform the dam operators (PSPC) of local conditions (provide on-the-ground observations and infrastructure concerns, provide resident and business feedback). And to coordinate local response (liaise information from the decision makers to residents, cottagers, business) – through communicating with the public (sandbagging, closures, advisories).”

So, lots of stakeholders and lots of sharing.

Watersheds

All of this is based on the watersheds.

In the beginning, there is the concept of an entire watershed to fathom.

It is an area of land that catches rain and snow, draining all precipitation—via surface runoff or groundwater—into a shared, common body of water. It is often described as a funnel.

So, the size of the watershed matters.

Using Lake Nipissing as an example, its major inflow is the Sturgeon River. This watershed of 7,000 square kilometres

It acts as a major headwater system for Lake Nipissing, with the river flowing 230 kilometres from the Lady Evelyn-Smoothwater Provincial Park area to its mouth on the north shore of Lake Nipissing.

But what people do not realize is that this runoff comes as far away as Anima-Nipissing Lake (and beyond) near Latchford, flows into the enormous water body of Lake Temagami, then Red Cedar Lake, then the Sturgeon River. There are all those creeks and ponds that are headwaters, flowing towards the main river.

Then some of this water must be discharged into the Upper and Lower French River and eventually Georgian Bay. So, you really must trace back where all this water originates.

If the ground is frozen or saturated, there is an excess of water. It must go somewhere.

Conservation Authorities

How does all this water regulation work? Especially since it wants to go where it wants.

CA’s in Ontario are community-based agencies mandated to protect people and property from natural hazards (like flooding) and to balance human, environmental, and economic needs. CAs may be consolidated into nine regions.

Governed by the Conservation Authorities Act, they manage natural resources on a watershed basis rather than by political boundaries.

Especially in spring, they issue advisories as to water levels. I contacted a few of them.

Get to know this link.

The Surface Water Monitoring Centre is the source of current flood and drought information in Ontario. We help protect you by providing information about potential flood and low water risks in your area, so that you can be prepared.

We learned about where the water flow data originates, a few weeks back, in the waterfall story. Otto Bédard filled us in. He is an ‘Expert Technologist - Hydraulics, Hydrometry and Innovation for Environment and Climate Change Canada,’ the Water Survey of Canada.

The Surface Water Monitoring Centre is part of a broader Early Warning System.

It monitors and collects data, as well as analyzes and forecasts water levels for the dissemination of timely messages to partners such as municipalities and conservation authorities.

You can receive data by logging in or using tools and maps, so you can get information on flood or low water risks in your area.

Carl Jorgensen is the long-time General Manager/Secretary-Treasurer of Conservation Sudbury. He explained why there are so many intricacies during this time of the year.

There are about a dozen dams and then several berms and weirs; a total of about 20 structures.

“Only the two dams owned and operated by the CA were purposely built for flood mitigation, as are our berms and some of the weirs.“

Many of the smaller dams serve only to maintain water levels. “A few dams control water levels for hydroelectric facilities; some can assist in flood mitigation and are actively managed that way during the freshet.

He said all dams are required to operate within the parameters of the Water Management Plans (WMP) under the auspices of the MNR. The tally above does not include things like tailing management dams found within mining operational areas.

He explained, “At this time of year, we are meeting several times a week with the dam operators and in between these meetings, there are numerous touch points. The CA within its jurisdiction has the mandated responsibility to provide flood forecasting and warning services to its municipality, so locally that means the City of Greater Sudbury. We also connect with local First Nations when their communities may be impacted by water levels.”

Then from Anjum Amin, P.Eng., Water Resources Engineer, Sault Ste. Marie Region Conservation Authority (SSMRCA).

The SSMRCA owns, operates, and maintains the Fort Creek Dam and Reservoir as well as five ‘Flood Control Channels.’

As an example, the drainage area for the Fort Creek dam is approximately 7.7 square kilometres, while the reservoir surface area is 3.24 hectares. The Fort Creek Flood Control Channel is 0.46 km in length and was dedicated in 1971.

The dam is an earthen structure with a low-permeability clay core confined by shells of granular material. The dam is 12 metres high and 117 metres long.

Amin said the five flood control channels are “referenced as engineered open-channel and diversion systems, not sluiceways in the dam sense. They were constructed to convey flood flows/stormwater safely from the northern and central parts of the watershed through the developed areas of the city and ultimately to the St. Mary’s River.” These channels form part of the city‑wide flood mitigation system.

Flood control services are provided through debris removal, grass cutting, maintenance, and repairs to the flood control structures. “This maintenance is essential to ensure the unobstructed flow of water.”

The North Bay-Mattawa CA (NMCA) asked about my social media posts regarding high water levels on the Mattawa River. They were interested in establishing some historic timelines based on the date stamps of the photos.

From Angela Mills, Water Resources Specialist, on April 24. “If you have dates and times for when flooding or receding began at Samuel de Champlain Park, it will help us establish criteria for issuing flood bulletins for the Mattawa River with the new gauge that has been installed in the last couple of years. If you have any date-times for beginning of minor flooding or what would be considered bank-full, as well as when it is potentially damaging or inundating areas that shouldn’t be part of the river, that would also be helpful.” I complied.

Beyond helping, the NBMCA has a unique structure. It does not operate any dams that control the water level on lakes or the flow on rivers.

“NBMCA has one water control structure, but it’s not a dam in the traditional sense, she said. “It’s a back flood control structure located on Parks Creek, which is a relatively low-lying and flat part of town surrounded by residential and commercial properties. The structure is only operated when Lake Nipissing water levels are high, with the purpose of preventing high water on Lake Nipissing from flooding back up Parks Creek and ensuring local stormwater infrastructure can function. When water levels get too high, stop logs are inserted, and then the creek is pumped around the structure to maintain flow to the lake.” That’s different.

Communication is key. She explained, “There are two committees that come together during freshet or other times of year as needed. One for the Sturgeon-Nipissing-French watershed, and one for the Upper Ottawa River watershed. These committees are hosted by the MNR and include: Dam operators (MNR (Surface water monitoring centre and local districts), OPG, Public Services and Procurement Canada, and private generating stations), the Ottawa River Regulation Secretariat (for Ottawa River only), the CA, and representatives from First Nations communities and Municipalities to get information directly from dam operators, relaying on the ground conditions in their local areas, and providing an opportunity for discussion that influences water management strategy.

“There is a pre-freshet meeting to bring everyone together, followed by regularly scheduled meetings once freshet begins that are held as needed depending on conditions on the landscape. Last week (earlier in April), while Lake Nipissing was rising rapidly, these meetings occurred daily, including over the weekend.

CA’s are responsible for the Flood Forecast and Warning program. Get to know the differences between Flood Warning (imminent) and Flood Risk (potential for).

Insurance

Things you should know about flooding and your home insurance coverage.

Floods are Canada’s most frequent and costliest natural disasters.

It is the greatest climate risk facing Canadians. In 2025, flooding caused over 1 billion in insured losses; in 2024, it caused over 4 billion in insured losses.

Up until 2015, comprehensive flood insurance was unavailable to Canadian homeowners. Canadian insurers generally provided coverage for water damage caused by indoor plumbing issues, malfunction of appliances, and sewer backups, but not for water damage caused by overland flooding, which is defined as “water entering a home through windows, doors and cracks.

Now you can often add optional "overland flood" or "water damage" endorsements to your policy, though coverage may be expensive, limited, or unavailable in high-risk zones. If you are in one of those areas, insurance may be difficult to arrange.

Standard home insurance policies typically do not cover overland flooding (water flowing into your home from outside), even if you live in a flood plain.

Andrew Bartucci, Director, External Communications for the Insurance Bureau of Canada, provided several links to the Insurance Bureau of Canada. Flood coverage may be optional. Have a look at the link and your policies. There are differences between flooding, sewage backup and broken pipes.

He said homeowners are encouraged to reach out to their insurance representative.

“Insurers are part of the solution, helping homeowners reduce their flood risk by offering discounts or better coverage terms for proven flood‑protection measures like backwater valves and sump pumps, and by rebuilding homes more resiliently after a loss.

“However, insurance alone can’t solve Canada’s flood problem. We must do more to protect homes, so they don’t flood in the first place. All levels of government can do more to protect new and existing homes from flooding.”

While 77 per cent of property insurers now offer overland flood coverage, its adoption remains low, leaving many with uncovered losses.

Municipal insurance coverage often includes extensions for flood damage to municipal buildings and infrastructure, as part of a broader risk management strategy. If there is a declared emergency and a fund to apply for relief, your insurance claims usually must be settled first, before applying.

There is a Disaster Financial Assistance Arrangements (DFAA) program, which provides financial assistance to provincial and territorial governments for response and recovery costs from large-scale disasters caused by natural hazards.

The DFAA program does not provide funding directly to Canadians impacted by disasters; this assistance is delivered by provinces and territories. The federal program helps provinces and territories cover the costs associated with disaster recovery. It was updated on April 1, 2025.

Finally

It has been a detailed story.

Spring brings warmer temperatures, melting snow, and heavy rainfall to the urban and back roads.

While these seasonal changes are a welcome sign of the end of winter, they also increase the risk of flooding. Many professionals are trying to mitigate the effects, and many others are affected by high water levels that go beyond.

Damn those dams? Not really. Dams are only one of the variables.

Naturally, there are others, and over time, we only have ourselves to blame.
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: PVWMA Releases Pajaro River Watershed Resilience Plan - TPG Online Daily
item.description:
By Jon Chown

A new plan to prepare the Pajaro River watershed for intensifying droughts, floods and other environmental pressures has been released by the Pajaro Valley Water Management Agency, and it calls for additional tax revenue.

The Pajaro River Watershed Resilience Plan, developed through the Pajaro River Watershed Resilience Program, lays out a comprehensive strategy to strengthen climate resilience across the watershed, which stretches through Santa Cruz, Monterey, San Benito and Santa Clara counties.

The watershed was selected in 2024 by the California Department of Water Resources as one of five pilot regions for its Watershed Resilience Program, an initiative designed to test new approaches to managing water resources and climate risks at a broader geographic scale. Funding for the planning effort was provided through the state budget.

The plan reflects input from a wide array of stakeholders, including public agencies, tribal representatives, farmers, environmental organizations, nonprofits and local residents. More than 700 participants contributed to the effort through what organizers described as a “watershed network,” with 15 entities serving on an advisory group. The result is a detailed plan to address a range of challenges, including flooding, prolonged drought, groundwater depletion, habitat loss and declining water quality.

“The PRWRP reflects the shared commitment of watershed partners to work toward coordinated, multi-benefit solutions for climate resilience in the watershed,” said Brian Lockwood, general manager of the Pajaro Valley Water Management Agency. “By aligning priorities and working together, we strengthen the region’s ability to acquire funding, implement projects, and deliver meaningful benefits to communities, agriculture, and ecosystems.”

Climate Threats Intensifying

The plan arrives as the Pajaro River watershed faces mounting evidence of climate stress.

In recent years, the region has experienced a series of extreme weather events, including major droughts, intense wildfires, floods due to atmospheric rivers and coastal flooding driven by sea level rise. These events have exposed vulnerabilities in not just the infrastructure, but land use patterns as well.

One of the most significant recent disasters occurred in March 2023, when a levee along the Pajaro River failed, sending floodwaters into the community of Pajaro. Approximately 3,500 residents, many of them farmworkers, were displaced, and 278 homes were inundated. The flooding caused millions of dollars in damage to infrastructure and agriculture and released an estimated 800,000 gallons of untreated sewage into the river system, which ultimately drains into Monterey Bay.

Officials say the event served as a stark reminder of the need for proactive investment in resilience.

“The levee failure alone underscored how urgently we need to reduce risk and invest in resilience before the next storm or extended drought,” the report states.

While photos and video of the flood captured public attention, the plan identifies drought as the most significant long-term threat to the watershed. A comprehensive vulnerability assessment of 525 assets across the region found that 42% are highly vulnerable to drought conditions, far higher than for any other climate hazard. Wildfire and sea level rise each accounted for 19% of high vulnerabilities, followed by extreme heat at 13% and flooding at 7%.

The findings reflect the region’s heavy dependence on groundwater. Approximately 90% of the watershed’s water supply comes from underground sources, and about 84% is used for agriculture, which is the economic backbone of the area.

The plan also points out that past droughts in the region have lasted as long as 13 years, far longer than those observed in modern records. When combined with future climate projections, those extended drought scenarios could become even more intense, increasing long-term drought frequency by as much as 30% to 100%.

A System Under Strain

The Pajaro River watershed is shaped by a complex network of rivers and tributaries, including Pacheco, Llagas, Uvas, San Benito and Salsipuedes-Corralitos creeks. These waterways originate in surrounding mountain ranges and flow into the Pajaro River, which serves as the region’s primary drainage system.

Most of the watershed’s precipitation comes from a small number of intense atmospheric river storms, creating a pattern of extreme variability. Wet years can bring significant flooding, while dry years can lead to severe water shortages.

This variability, combined with the effects of climate change, presents a growing challenge for water managers.

In the report, longtime grower Peter Van Dyke, whose family has farmed in the region for generations, described how the landscape has changed over time. He pointed to shifts toward more water-intensive crops, such as strawberries, and the impact of fast-moving stormwater on farmland, noting that upstream development has altered natural water flows and increased runoff.

“The watershed has taken a beating,” he said during the planning process, according to the report.

The watershed’s vulnerabilities extend beyond water supply. Decades of development in both flood-prone and water-stressed areas, along with the channelization of rivers and loss of natural floodplains, have increased exposure to flooding and reduced the landscape’s ability to absorb and store water.

Urban expansion into the wildland-urban interface has heightened wildfire risk, while agricultural practices and changing land use patterns have contributed to soil degradation and increased pressure on groundwater.

Water quality is also under threat, particularly in coastal areas where seawater intrusion is contaminating groundwater supplies. Rising sea levels are expected to exacerbate the problem, pushing saltwater further inland and raising groundwater tables, which could lead to additional flooding and infrastructure damage.

From Planning to Action

To address these challenges, planners identified 13 “Keystone Actions” as top priorities. These initiatives were selected based on their ability to address multiple vulnerabilities at once, deliver benefits across different systems and communities, and advance equity — particularly for historically underserved populations and tribal communities.

Among the proposed solutions are large-scale floodplain restoration projects designed to reconnect rivers with their natural floodplains. Restoring wetlands and riparian corridors can provide multiple benefits including flood protection, groundwater recharge and habitat restoration. Other strategies include expanding managed aquifer recharge, increasing the use of recycled water, and implementing conservation measures to reduce demand.

In urban areas, the plan calls for “green streets” and low-impact development approaches that capture and infiltrate stormwater, helping to reduce runoff and improve water quality.

Agricultural land stewardship is another key focus, with recommendations aimed at improving soil health, increasing water efficiency and exploring voluntary land repurposing in areas that are no longer sustainable for intensive farming.

A central theme of the plan is the importance of equity and inclusion, particularly in relation to tribal communities and underserved populations.

The plan calls for increased tribal access to culturally significant lands and the development of co-stewardship agreements that incorporate traditional ecological knowledge into land and water management practices. It also highlights the need to improve public access to outdoor spaces, which are increasingly threatened by climate impacts such as extreme heat, wildfire and coastal flooding.

However, the plan acknowledges that implementing all of these plans will be difficult as many agencies and community organizations face limited staffing, funding and technical ability.

Funding the Future

One of the most significant challenges identified in the plan is how to pay for the extensive work required. To address this, the plan proposes a mix of philanthropic fundraising and increased taxes.

In the first phase, covering roughly the first two years, funding would focus on building organizational capacity, including staffing, project development and early technical studies. This stage would rely on a combination of local agency contributions, nonprofit support and philanthropic funding.

The second phase, spanning years two through four, would focus on securing state and federal grants to support planning, design and construction of projects. The final phase, beginning around year four and continuing long term, would require what the agency terms as “stable, locally controlled funding sources” to support operations, maintenance and monitoring. Basically, this means new taxes or increased taxes. Potential options include increasing rates for water use, parcel-based fees, development impact fees, or other voter-approved measures.

The report says establishing “reliable funding streams” will be critical to ensuring that projects can be maintained and adapted over time.

The plan concludes with a call to action, emphasizing that the time to act is now. Without coordinated efforts, the report warns, climate impacts will continue to intensify, placing increasing strain on communities, ecosystems and the regional economy.

“Action now can protect people, places, and livelihoods across the watershed,” the report states.
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Is Hantavirus a threat to Europe? Climate change and habitat destruction are increasing the risk
item.description:
Is Hantavirus a threat to Europe? Climate change and habitat destruction are increasing the risk

Fears of another global pandemic erupted this month after the MV Hondius cruise ship became the centre of a deadly hantavirus outbreak.

The latest World Health Organization (WHO) Disease Outbreak News report states that as of 13 May, a total of 11 cases, including three deaths, have been reported.

More cases are expected to arise given the dynamics of spread on a ship and the virus’ incubation period. However, the WHO confirms that there are no signs of a “larger outbreak” at the current time.

What is hantavirus and how does it spread?

Headlines of self-isolating passengers and images of ambulance workers dressed in PPE have triggered comparisons to the COVID pandemic. It’s a worry the WHO has repeatedly shut down, citing the varying nature of how the two diseases are transmitted.

“This is not coronavirus,” said Maria van Kerkhove, director of epidemic and pandemic preparedness at the WHO, following news of the first passenger fatality. “I want to be unequivocal here. This is not SARS-CoV-2 and not the start of a COVID pandemic.”

Hantaviruses, which are named after a river in South Korea, describe a family of viruses rather than a single disease. There are more than 20 different hantaviruses, almost all of which are linked to infection by rodents such as rats and mice.

Related

While most hantaviruses cannot be passed from person to person, rare instances of human transmission have been documented with the Andes virus strain, which is the particular strain behind the cruise ship outbreak.

However, transmission still only occurs through close contact. In 2018, for example, there was an Argentinian outbreak of the virus that was traced back to a party. A single person with the virus is thought to have spread it to 34 people, with 11 deaths.

Is climate change to blame for the hantavirus outbreak?

Climate change and infectious diseases have long been linked by scientists.

The climate is an important factor for the distribution and spread of organisms, including disease hosts (animals that can harbour or transmit diseases), vectors (arthropod animals like mosquitoes and ticks that transmit diseases) and pathogens (the microorganisms that cause the disease itself, such as viruses or bacteria).

According to a 2022 study published in the science journal Nature, more than half of all human infectious diseases are climate sensitive, including hantaviruses.

“Whenever a disease is climate sensitive, there is the potential for climate change to be influencing its epidemiology, including its distribution and impacts on people,” Kris Murray, a professor at Medical Research Council Unit The Gambia at the London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine, tells Euronews Earth.

“In the case of hantavirus, climate characteristics and climate change may directly influence the presence or abundance of hantavirus host species, such as numerous rodent species.”

A change in rainfall patterns, for example, can influence the reproductive timing and success of rodent host species. Murray warns that this can have “downstream impacts” on human exposure.

Habitat destruction can fuel zoonotic disease outbreaks

Habitat loss, destruction or degradation – often caused by human-caused deforestation – can both directly and indirectly influence disease transmission.

“In areas where zoonotic hosts occur, removal of vegetation or other destructive activities can mobilise infectious pathogens,” Murray explains.

“Hantavirus for instance is often caused by people disturbing areas where rodents (the natural reservoir host group of hantaviruses) occur, since hantavirus is shed in their droppings and urine, which can persist in the environment for some time.”

When these environments are disturbed, the pathogen can be aerosolized, meaning people in the vicinity who breathe it in may become infected.

“Fragmented ecosystems often favour adaptable reservoir species such as rodents, bats or ticks, while reducing the natural ecological balances that help regulate pathogen transmissions,” Professor Jörg Schelling, former Director of the Institute of General Medicine at the University Hospital of LMU Munich, tells Euronews Earth.

The WHO is currently working on the hypothesis that transmissions occurred before passengers boarded. Since July last year, Argentina has recorded 101 hantavirus cases, with 32 deaths. This is significantly higher than 2024-2025, when 64 cases and 14 deaths were reported.

Experts have warned that the increase follows the significant drought that hit Argentina in 2023 and 2024, which was then followed by increased rainfall in the subsequent years. This means more vegetation cover and more food for hantavirus hosts such as rats.

Is hantavirus a threat to Europe?

Hantavirus existed in Europe long before it dominated headlines at the beginning of this month, with the first documented outbreak occurring in Sweden in 1934.

A 2009 study published in the National Library of Medicine says that elevated temperatures in West-Central Europe have been associated with more frequent Puumala hantavirus outbreaks through high seed production and high bank vole densities.

On the other hand, warm winters in Scandinavia have led to a decline in vole populations as a result of the missing protective snow cover.

Puumala is the most common cause of hantavirus infections in Europe and can spread to people through the inhalation of airborne dust contaminated by the urine, droppings or saliva of infected bank voles. It causes a mild form of hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome, with symptoms spanning from sudden fever and headaches to back and abdominal pain.

However, it is rarely fatal, and cannot be spread from human to human.

Related

“Hantaviruses do occur globally, including in Europe, and it’s conceivable that climate change could create new or increasing opportunities for spillover into people but more work is required to understand the risks at the individual host species level,” Murray explains.

“Importantly for hantaviruses, while they do cause occasional illness in people across the world, they do not generally exhibit strong human-human transmission, and it’s really this that differentiates rare sporadic and usually fairly isolated cases from larger clusters of infected people as we’re seeing in the current outbreak.”

Schelling argues that climate projections suggest that parts of Northern and Western Europe in particular may become “increasingly suitable” for rodent species that act as hantavirus reservoirs.

“Regions that historically experienced colder climates – including parts of Scandinavia, the Baltic region, and higher-altitude areas in Central Europe – may see longer transmission seasons and changing patterns of rodent abundance as temperatures rise,” he says.

Schelling adds that while uncertainty remains regarding the exact geographic shifts of hantavirus, Europe’s zoonotic disease landscape is likely to change “substantially” over the coming decades.

What can Europe do to tackle hantavirus?

Following the hantavirus outbreak, experts are calling on policymakers to strengthen surveillance systems that combine epidemiological, ecological and climate data.

“This includes monitoring variables such as temperature, precipitation, vegetation indices, land-use change and biodiversity indicators alongside human disease surveillance,” Schelling says.

“At a national level, some countries already use climate-informed forecasting for vector-borne diseases such as Dengue, Chikungunya, West Nile virus or TBE (tick-borne encephalitis), which can serve as models for broader surveillance.”

Schelling argues that public health systems across Europe still need more investment in climate-resilient infrastructure, pointing out that the prevention of future outbreaks depends not just on better surveillance, but on addressing the root causes of ecological disruption and climate change itself.

The International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) says the recent outbreak of hantavirus has revealed an “unexpected solution”: restoring nature.

A 2021 study published in Science Direct found that restoring tropical forest landscapes could reduce populations of two major reservoir rodent species, lowering transmission risk for nearly 2.8 million people living in vulnerable regions.

“Restoration should be recognised as a public health intervention,” says Paula Prist of IUCN. “While its role in mitigating climate change and restoring biodiversity is well established, it is also a vital strategy for protecting human health.”

Moving forward, Murray says further research is required to better understand outbreak risk, particularly for “emerging infections that are either newly appearing in the human population or doing things that seem different to usual”.
